steps.title: State what is to be proved
steps.body:
**Claim:** the sum of any two odd numbers is even.

We must show this for *every* pair of odd numbers, not just a few examples — so we need proof by deduction, starting from a general algebraic representation of an odd number.

steps.title: Represent the odd numbers algebraically
steps.body: Let the two odd numbers be $2m + 1$ and $2n + 1$, where $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (this is the general form of *any* odd integer, so the argument will cover every possible pair).

steps.title: Factor out 2
steps.body:
$$
2m + 2n + 2 = 2(m + n + 1).
$$

Since $m$ and $n$ are integers, $m + n + 1$ is also an integer. Call it $k = m + n + 1 \in \mathbb{Z}$.

steps.title: Recognise the form of an even number
steps.body: The sum equals $2k$ for some integer $k$. By definition, any integer of the form $2k$ (with $k \in \mathbb{Z}$) is even.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.body: Therefore $(2m+1) + (2n+1)$ is always even, for **every** choice of integers $m$ and $n$. This completes the proof by deduction — the chain of algebraic steps applies to all odd numbers simultaneously, which is exactly why it counts as a proof rather than a check of a few cases. $\blacksquare$
